lang.name: English
meta.title: The Monty Hall Problem: Try It Yourself
header.title: The Monty Hall Problem: Try It Yourself
header.sub: Should you switch doors, or stay? Try it and see for yourself.
steps.title: How to play (4 steps)
steps.s2: The host opens a losing door
game.intro_html:
Welcome! Behind just one of these 3 doors is a new car. The other 2 hide goats.
To start, click any door you like.
game.picked_html:
You picked Door {n}.
I know where the car is. Let me open one of the other doors and show you a goat...
game.decide_html:
As you can see, Door {o} had a goat.
So, what will you do? Stay with Door {p}, the one you picked first? Or switch to the other closed door, Door {c}?
game.win: 🎉 You win! Door {n} had the new car!
game.lose: 😢 Too bad... Door {n} had a goat. The new car was behind Door {c}.
game.afterSwitch_html:
You switched doors. The result has been added to the "Your record" table.
Play a few more times and you'll start to see which plan wins more often.
game.afterStay_html:
You stayed with your door. The result has been added to the "Your record" table.
Play a few more times and you'll start to see which plan wins more often.
record.title: Your record
record.lead: Play a few rounds. Does switching win more often than staying?
sim.title: Let the computer try it many times
sim.lead: Playing by hand gives you only a few rounds, so luck has a big say. Let's have the computer play the same game over and over. It tries the switch plan and the stay plan the same number of times, then compares how often each one wins.
sim.statusInit: Press a button and the results will pile up here.
sim.statusRan: Added {n} more rounds of each ({t} of each in total). Watch whether the bars move closer to the dotted lines.
sim.statusCleared: The record has been cleared. Let's try again.
sim.chartTitle: Win rate (%) — the dotted lines mark "1 time in 3" (33.3%) and "2 times in 3" (66.7%)
why.title: Why does switching win more often?
why.lead_html: Say you picked Door 1. The car can be in one of 3 places, and each is just as likely. The host knows where it is, and always opens a door with a goat.
why.case1_html:
Case 1: the car is behind Door 1
Switch, and you get a goat — you lose / Stay, and you win
why.case2_html:
Case 2: the car is behind Door 2
The host can only open Door 3, so switching takes you to Door 2 — you win
why.case3_html:
Case 3: the car is behind Door 3
The host can only open Door 2, so switching takes you to Door 3 — you win
why.concl_html: The switch plan wins in 2 of the 3 cases. The stay plan wins in only 1. That is why switching wins twice as often. It is also why the bars in the simulation above creep toward the 66.7% and 33.3% dotted lines.
ext.title: Try more doors
ext.lead_html: With 3 doors it may still feel like a 50-50 guess. So let's add more doors. Imagine picking 1 door out of 100, and then the host opens 98 doors with goats behind them. Would you still hold on to your first pick?
ext.customAria: Type your own number of doors
ext.modeAll: Open doors until only 1 closed door is left
ext.modeOne: Open just 1 door with a goat
ext.intro_html:
There are {n} doors. Only 1 hides the new car; the other {m} hide goats.
Pick any door you like.
ext.opening_html: You picked Door {p}. I know where the car is. Now I'll open {k} doors with goats behind them...
ext.decideAll_html:
I opened {k} doors with goats. The only doors still closed are your Door {p} and Door {c}.
Click Door {c} to switch. If you'd rather keep your door, press "Stay with this door".
ext.decideOne_html:
I opened just 1 door with a goat. That leaves {c} doors still closed.
To switch, click one of the closed doors. If you'd rather keep your door, press "Stay with this door".
ext.afterSwitch_html: You switched doors. The result has been added to the table below.
ext.afterStay_html: You stayed with your door. The result has been added to the table below.
ext.win: 🎉 You win! Door {n} had the new car!
ext.lose: 😢 Too bad... Door {n} had a goat. The new car was behind Door {c}.
ext.sim100: Try 100 more times with these settings
ext.sim1000: Try 1000 more times with these settings
ext.simInit: Let the computer repeat the game with the same settings, and check whether the results match the numbers we worked out.
ext.simRan: Added {n} rounds with {d} doors and "{mode}" ({t} of each in total).
ext.chartTitle: Win rate (%) — the dotted lines show the numbers we worked out (switch {s}% / stay {y}%)
ext.theoryAll_html: Your very first pick wins only 1 time in {n} ({ty}%). The other {m}/{n} of the time the car is behind some other door, and the host avoids it, opening only doors with goats. So in those cases, the other door still closed at the end hides the car. That is why switching wins {s}% of the time.
ext.theoryOne3_html: With 3 doors, once the host opens 1 door only 1 closed door is left. This is exactly the basic game above: switching wins {s}% of the time, staying {ty}%.
ext.theoryOneN_html: If the host opens only 1 door, {c} doors stay closed. Even if you switch, you can't tell which of them hides the car. Switching wins {s}% of the time, staying {ty}%. Switching is still the better choice, but the more doors there are, the smaller the gap becomes.
